title: Deadpan Parking Receipt
summary: A character reports an impossible event with no emotional reaction.
scenario: A normal errand scene is interrupted by a wildly impossible statement delivered calmly.
exampleText: The refrigerator has become legally responsible for the garage. Anyway, we are out of mustard.
whyItWorks: The absurdity is high while the visible emotional reaction is low, creating the deadpan mismatch.
howToUse: Use this pattern when a scene needs a clean absurdity spike without characters winking at the audience.
boundaryNote: Comedy craft pattern only; not a claim about real emotional health.
misuseWarning: Do not use deadpan examples to dismiss real distress or emotional communication.

title: The Group Chat Weather Event
summary: A read receipt becomes the seed of an escalating invented story.
scenario: A character sees that a message was read but not answered, then begins filling the silence with assumptions.
exampleText: The message had been seen for fourteen minutes. In group-chat time, this was no longer silence. This was a weather system.
whyItWorks: Assumptions rise while evidence stays tiny, so the emotional story grows faster than the facts.
howToUse: Use this pattern when a scene needs tension created by uncertainty rather than confirmed betrayal.
boundaryNote: Story-design lens only; do not use to mock anxiety, relational pain, or real uncertainty.
misuseWarning: Do not frame someone’s fear or worry as automatically silly or invalid.

title: Fear Signal Reflection
summary: A gentle prompt that separates known threat, imagined threat, and available support.
scenario: A user or character feels fear rising because the next step is uncertain.
exampleText: What is known, what is imagined, and what support would make the next step safer? Name one safety signal already present.
whyItWorks: The prompt identifies threat, uncertainty, and imagination while inviting safety signals, knowledge, and support.
howToUse: Use this as an optional journaling or character-design prompt when fear needs clarity rather than dismissal.
boundaryNote: Reflection invitation only; not clinical guidance. If danger is immediate, real-world safety comes first.
misuseWarning: Do not use this prompt to minimize fear or pressure someone to calm down before they feel safe.

title: The Shopkeeper Trust Arc
summary: An NPC becomes warmer when the player returns, repairs, and acts consistently.
scenario: A cozy shopkeeper remembers whether the player keeps promises and repairs mistakes.
exampleText: Low trust: 'I have seen promises walk out that door before.' High trust: 'You came back. That matters more than you think.'
whyItWorks: Attention, care, trust, and time make the relationship feel earned instead of automatic.
howToUse: Use this pattern for NPC relationships where kindness, consistency, and repair change future dialogue.
boundaryNote: NPC design logic only; not a real relationship score.
misuseWarning: Do not turn trust into manipulation math or use love language to excuse control or harm.

title: The Boundary Repair Scene
summary: A character names a broken agreement without turning anger into harm.
scenario: Two characters have repeated conflict because one boundary keeps getting crossed.
exampleText: I am angry because the agreement mattered. I do not want to punish you. I want the line to be real this time.
whyItWorks: The scene separates harm, violation, and need from clarity and repair, keeping the boundary visible.
howToUse: Use this for character arcs where anger becomes a signal for clean action rather than escalation.
boundaryNote: Creative conflict-design example only; not advice for unsafe real-world conflict.
misuseWarning: Do not use anger language to justify threats, coercion, or harm.

title: The Memory Object
summary: A grief scene uses an object to let memory be witnessed instead of solved.
scenario: A character finds an ordinary object connected to someone they lost.
exampleText: The mug was still chipped on the handle. Nobody fixed it. Somehow that was the part that made the room honest.
whyItWorks: Love, absence, and memory intensify the echo, while ritual, witness, and time create a holding structure.
howToUse: Use this for gentle grief scenes where the story pauses to honor memory without forcing closure.
boundaryNote: Reflection/craft example only; not grief therapy.
misuseWarning: Do not rush, grade, or pathologize grief with this pattern.

title: Wrong Answers Welcome
summary: A learning activity turns uncertainty into permission to explore.
scenario: A class or workshop gets stuck because everyone is afraid of being wrong.
exampleText: Write three wrong answers first. Circle the one that teaches us the most. Now we can begin.
whyItWorks: Mystery, safety, and permission increase curiosity while fear of being wrong is softened.
howToUse: Use this to design a low-shame opening move for workshops, classrooms, or tutorial scenes.
boundaryNote: Education/design prompt only; adapt to the real learners and setting.
misuseWarning: Do not use forced vulnerability or public mistakes as humiliation.

title: The Secret Door That Was Always There
summary: A game rewards patient curiosity with a small, beautiful discovery.
scenario: The player returns to a familiar room after gaining a new way to notice details.
exampleText: The wall did not open because of a key. It opened because the player finally stopped running past the painted moth.
whyItWorks: Curiosity, beauty, and mystery rise when cynicism and hurry drop, creating a gentle wonder reward.
howToUse: Use this for cozy exploration mechanics where attention is rewarded without punishing the player.
boundaryNote: Game-design pattern only; not a claim that wonder fixes pain.
misuseWarning: Do not use wonder language to bypass grief, fear, or real needs.
